identified_error: The paper appears to have treated repeated symptom reports/individual symptoms as independent observations in the Kaplan-Meier/Cox time-to-resolution analyses, rather than analyzing time to resolution at the child level (or otherwise accounting for within-child correlation and the fact that most children did not have PCC by the WHO definition). This is especially evident because only 1 child reportedly met the WHO PCC definition, yet the paper reports numerous highly significant hazard ratios for multiple symptoms and broad conclusions about 'post-COVID-19 symptoms' and their resolution. With essentially no true PCC events, those survival/Cox estimates are not a valid basis for inference about PCC.
why_fatal: If only 1 child met the stated PCC definition, there are effectively no data to support stable survival or Cox regression estimates about predictors of PCC symptom resolution. Any model producing multiple precise, highly significant hazard ratios in that setting is methodologically invalid or based on the wrong unit of analysis. This invalidates the central conclusions that pre-COVID symptoms predict delayed post-COVID symptom resolution and that the study estimates PCC incidence meaningfully, because the inferential statistics do not correspond to the stated outcome definition and are driven by mis-specified analysis rather than genuine events.
correct_approach: The analysis should have been aligned with the defined outcome and unit of observation. If the target outcome was WHO-defined PCC, the authors should have restricted analysis to children who actually met that definition; with only 1 case, they should not have fit Cox models at all and should have reported descriptive data only. If they instead wanted to study duration of any post-infection symptom, they needed to redefine the outcome explicitly, analyze at the child level or use methods for repeated measures/multistate data that account for multiple symptoms within the same child, and avoid making claims about PCC when the data only support analysis of short-term post-acute symptoms.
additional_errors: Outcome definition mismatch: the paper's conclusions about PCC/long COVID are inconsistent with the fact that only 1 child met the WHO definition.
Possible severe overfitting/insufficient events per variable in Cox models.
Potential failure to account for censoring and clustered symptom episodes within children.
Use of parent-reported common viral symptoms (rhinitis, sore throat, cough, fever) without robust attribution to SARS-CoV-2 may cause outcome misclassification.
The definition of symptom resolution using nonconsecutive symptom-free weeks is unconventional and may bias time-to-resolution estimates.
